coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and DFT study of IFE based nano-sensor for fluorometric detection of Norepinephrine
coredata.dc:creator: Duhan, Jyoti
Kumar, Himanshu
Obrai, Sangeeta
coredata.dc:description: Norepinephrine act as both neurotransmitter and hormones in the central neurological and cardiovascular systems of mammals. According to literature reports, standard methods for detecting NEP like spectrophotometry, electrochemistry, chemiluminescence, capillary electrophoresis and high-performance liquid chromatography etc. are expensive, time-consuming and have poor resolution especially in aqueous or biological systems. In light of this, we have fabricated the boron phosphorus graphene quantum dots (BPGQDs) nanosensor for the fluorometric detection of NEP in pure aqueous conditions. The designed BPGQDs were characterized by using UV–VIS absorption spectroscopy, FT-IR, PXRD, HR-TEM, SEM, EDS, AFM, XPS, Fluorescence spectroscopy and TCS-PC. The excitation wavelength of nanosensor was 340 nm and emission wavelength were 420nm, with the addition of NEP to the nanosensor the initial fluorescence intensity gets quenched and the quenching mechanism was inner filter effect. A smartphone-based sensor for the detection of NEP using BPGQDs was also developed. Our experimental results also prove with density functional theory by calculating HOMO, LUMO energy, optimization geometry and overall energy of the system. The fabricated sensor revealed high selectivity towards NEP with low limit of detection (LOD) 16.2 nM and Limit of quantification was 49 nM. Further the synthesized sensor was also applied for real time analysis of NEP in human urine samples.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Shows HR-TEM image of synthesized BPGQDs (b) histogram plots for average particle size of BPGQDs (c) powder XRD spectra (d) absorption and excitation spectra of acceptor and donor (e) AFM image and (f) FT-IR spectra of BPGQDs.

(a) XPS spectra of BPGQDs (b) High resolved spectra of B1s (c) High resolution spectra of C1s (d) High resolution spectra of O1s (e) High resolution XPS spectra of P2p and (f) TCS-PC graph of BPGQDs and mix of BPGQDs + NEP.

(a) shows the effect of pH on fluorescence intensities of BPGQDs (b) Effects of different excitation wavelength on fluorescence intensities of BPGQDs (c) Quenching of fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs with addition of NEP (d) linear stern-volmer plot between F0/F and conc. Of NEP (e) CIE plots of fluorescence spectra for BPGQDs with different conc. Of NEP.
Graphical representation of the HOMO-LUMO energy orbitals of BPGQDs and Norepinephrine.
(a) Selectivity plot for detection of NEP (b) selective detection of NEP in presence of any other species.

(a) Schematic representation of smartphone based portable sensing plastform. (b) the relationship of (R + G + B)/3 values with increasing concentration of NEP(insert shows the images of BPGQDs and BPGQDs + NEP in UV lamp).
Pictorial representation of fabrication of BPGQDs and its quenching in presence of NEP.
Real sample analysis of NEP using BPGQDs.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and DFT study of IFE based nano-sensor for fluorometric detection of Norepinephrine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BR Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BR Ambedkar National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BR Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Norepinephrine act as both neurotransmitter and hormones in the central neurological and cardiovascular systems of mammals. According to literature reports, standard methods for detecting NEP like spectrophotometry, electrochemistry, chemiluminescence, capillary electrophoresis and high-performance liquid chromatography etc. are expensive, time-consuming and have poor resolution especially in aqueous or biological systems. In light of this, we have fabricated the boron phosphorus graphene quantum dots (BPGQDs) nanosensor for the fluorometric detection of NEP in pure aqueous conditions. The designed BPGQDs were characterized by using UV–VIS absorption spectroscopy, FT-IR, PXRD, HR-TEM, SEM, EDS, AFM, XPS, Fluorescence spectroscopy and TCS-PC. The excitation wavelength of nanosensor was 340 nm and emission wavelength were 420nm, with the addition of NEP to the nanosensor the initial fluorescence intensity gets quenched and the quenching mechanism was inner filter effect. A smartphone-based sensor for the detection of NEP using BPGQDs was also developed. Our experimental results also prove with density functional theory by calculating HOMO, LUMO energy, optimization geometry and overall energy of the system. The fabricated sensor revealed high selectivity towards NEP with low limit of detection (LOD) 16.2 nM and Limit of quantification was 49 nM. Further the synthesized sensor was also applied for real time analysis of NEP in human urine samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Norepinephrine (NEP) is an important neurotransmitter in the central neurological and circulatory systems of mammals [1]. Their aberrant concentration may be caused a number of illnesses including schizophrenia, Alzheimer's, Parkinsonism, pheochromocytoma, severe head trauma, different neuroblastomas and depression [2,3]. As the release of norepinephrine is greatly elevated under dangerous and stressful conditions mobilizing the body and brain for action, it is linked to the fight-or-flight response [4,5]. High levels of the neurotransmitter increase anxiety, thyroid hormone shortage and congestive heart failure whereas low levels are linked to depression and postural hypotension [6,7]. In order to diagnose diseases early and conduct pathological research, sensitive analysis of NEP anomalies is useful. Now a days NEP attracted attention as tumor biomarkers and due to erratic noradrenaline levels can cause organ failure from excessively high blood pressure which causes heart attacks and kidney damage. Numerous analytical approaches have been used to establish the concentrations of noradrenaline in biological materials because of its importance as a biomarker. The most popular techniques used for detection of NEP are spectrophotometry [8], electrochemistry [9], chemiluminescence [10], high-performance liquid chromatography [11,12] and capillary electrophoresis [13] have been developed. However, several of the techniques have drawbacks in terms of cost-effectiveness, sensitivity and complexity because of their small concentration, ease of oxidation and comparable structures [14]. Due to their ease, high sensitivity and efficiency in comparison to other tactics these limitations led to increased interest in fluorescence-based measuring strategies. Therefore, it is vitally required to create easy, simple, focused, and sensitive approach for NEP sensing.

Carbon-based semiconductor nanocrystals called graphene quantum dots have a size fewer than 10 nm, strong photoluminescence, low cost of manufacture and low toxicity [15,16]. They have received extensive research and are employed in a widespread range of applications, including the creation of LED, solar cells, chemo sensors, biosensing and in vivo protein tracking agents [17–21]. As a recent addition to the family of carbon materials and an ingredient in numerous fluorescence sensor materials, GQDs have captured the interest of numerous scientists. Traditional quantum dots are a threat to bio-safety and the environment since they contain synthetic components [22]. The benefits of GQDs however include minimal biocompatibility, biotoxicity, strong aquatic solubility and the ability to control light stability and fluorescence. Additionally, GQDs are environmentally acceptable materials with a relatively stable structure, light corrosion resistance and no highly hazardous metal components. Hence due to these properties, GQDs have been used as a biological imaging and sensors.

However, a number of methods have been devised to produce graphene quantum dots with adjustable optical properties including doping the particles with various atoms or utilizing various carbon precursors and synthesis media. Previously, GQDs were synthesized using “top-down” or “bottom-up” approaches comparable to those for other nanomaterials. Inexpensive carbon blends, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerenes, etc. can be used as starting materials for top-down approaches [23] but long synthesis time, inability to adjust experimental conditions, low yield and size spread are some of the limitations of the top-down approach. Smaller molecules were used as starting materials in the bottom-up method [24]. Compared with the top-down approach, this method provides a more controllable process with better control of optical properties, higher yields, and better carbonation [25,26]. The poor quantum yield of GQDs results remained a limitation for fluorometric biosensing applications. According to previous literature, the intrinsic properties of carbon nanomaterials can be tuned by heteroatom doping and co-doping. Several dopants including phosphorus, nitrogen [27], sulphur [28] and boron [29] have been doped in GQDs. Recently some studies showed successful preparation of NS co-doped GQDs [30], BN co-doped GQDs [31], NFS co-doped GQDs [32] and NP co-doped GQDs [33] and this co-doping modify high quantum yield, high fluorescent behaviour, more specific binding, electronic structure, chemical activity and quantum yield, thereby leading to better photoluminescence properties of GQDs. More studies are urgently need have undergone in-depth study, demonstrating a significant shift in the photophysical characteristics of GQDs. To improve the fluorescence of GQDs, various heteroatoms have been co-doped into them. A PN junction semiconductor with lower toxicity and very steady photoluminescence than un-doped GQDs is produced by such co-doping of GQDs with B and N [34]. Here, N is a nonmetal with abundant electrons, whereas B is a metalloid with few electrons. The remaining fifth electron in NGQDs is softly bound to the parental atom and forms bonds with the four neighboring C atoms as a result of the N atom's doping of GQDs. B has three valence electrons, causing electron vacancies also known as holes to be seen [35]. Close interaction between this manufactured p-type for BGQDs and n-type semiconductor for NGQDs and results in the formation of a PN junction. The PN junction configuration for B,N-GQDs has a gradient of concentration on both the P and N sides. In this instance, electrons and holes both diffuse from NGQDs to BGQDs [36]. Recombination with BGQDs has reduced this diffusion of holes on the NGQDs as well as the diffusion of electrons from the NGQDs. As a result, NGQDs develop a positive charge, while BGQDs develop a negative charge. This makes it possible for them to be used in energy storage, super capacitors, photocatalysis, biosensing and bioimaging. Compared to other nanomaterials, co-doping GQDs gives them a well platform for usage as a detecting material [37].

In this article, we present the fabrication of novel boron and phosphorus co-doped graphene quantum dots (BPGQDs) for the extremely sensitive and focused detection of NEP. We present the production of BPGQDs using one step hydrothermal technique and their sensory response towards NEP because as far as we know boron and phosphorus co-doped GQDs have not built yet. Upon the addition of NEP, the fluorescence of BPGQDs displayed a strong quenching reaction (scheme1 ). The mechanism of quenching was inner filter effects process between BPGQDs and quinone formed by the oxidation of NEP. The proposed sensing mechanism was examined using a brief study of Fluorescence, UV–VIS absorption and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. Theoretical studies also done by us in this work for studying the interactions mechanism and optimized geometry of novel BPGQDs. A smartphone-based sensor was also developed for detection of NEP using the newly synthesized BPGQDs. Real time analysis study was also observed using human urine samples.

Norepinephrine, dopamine, serotonin, tryptophan were bought from sigma Aldrich. Boric acid, phosphoric acid, citric acid, glycine, alanine, Ferric chloride, Zinc nitrate, Manganese Oxide were purchased from loba and all the chemicals are of analytical grade and used directly without any refinement. Ultra-pure purified water was used during the entire testing.

Citric acid(5 g) was dissolved in 10 ml of distilled water then add 2 g boric acid and 3 ml phosphoric acid to the above blend solution. Then, the mixed solution was put on magnetic stirrer up to 20 min with heating temperature 40 °C. Further, transfer the above solution in crucible and placed in muffle furnance at 180 °C for 6 h. The brown liquid solution was obtained after cooling to room temperature. The obtained solution was filtered and centrifuge with 12000 rpm and then, fill in dialysis bag for 48 h to eliminate bulky particles. At last, the obtained BPGQDs was stored for further characterization and application in refrigerator at 4 °C.

UV–VIS absorption spectra were recorded in UV–VIS spectrophotometer (Lab-India), fluorescence spectra were noted in Fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent technology). High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM), Scanning electron microscope (SEM), X-Ray diffraction (XRD), Energy dispersive spectrum (EDS), FT-IR Spectra, X-Ray photo electroscope (XPS) and atomic force microscope (AFM) were used for structural characterization of synthesized BPGQDs. Time correlated single photon count was used for studying fluorescence lifetime and for mechanism of quenching. Theoretical studies of synthesized materials were studied by using Gaussian16 software and I phone used as a smartphone study of materials.

NEP stock solution with concentration 10−6M were freshly prepared in distilled water then add 1 M NaOH solution to maintain (pH 10). Then prepare 10−4M solution of synthesized BPGQDs. For detection of NEP, different conc. Of NEP (3,6,9…75 nM) were added to the BPGQDs solution, and the mixed solutions put stable for 5 min at room temperature before noted the fluorescence spectra. For sensing of NEP in real samples, human urine samples were subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. In the subsequent Sensing methods, 1 % human urine solution was utilised in place of clean buffer solution. The 1 % human urine solution containing the BPGQDs solution was added with various final concentrations of NEP (5, 10,15 and 20 nM) and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded similarly.

The developed BPGQDs were ist characterized to confirm the optical and structural properties. By using HR-TEM the morphology and size of BPGQDs were studied. The HR-TEM images of BPGQDs shown in Fig. 1 (a) and we clearly found that synthesized materials were spherical and size of the particles lies in range 3–4 nm [38,39]. ImageJ software was used to calculate the average size of particles and calculated size was 3.2 nm as shown in Fig. 1(b). SEM analysis was also helps to analysis the morphology of particles, we found that particles are spherical and size lies at nanoscale shown in Fig.S1 [27]. In the XRD spectra of BPGQDs we found a peak at 230 that confirm the formation of graphitic like structure of BPGQDs as shown in Fig. 1(c) and broad peak proves the smaller size of particles which was consistent with other reports on GQDs [40,41]. A strong absorption peak at 332 nm in UV–VIS absorption spectrum was attributed to the n-π* transition of the C = O shown in Fig.S2 bond and the excitation spectrum for BPGQDs showed a peak at 340 nm that was consistent with the UV–VIS absorption spectrum of NEP shown in Fig. 1(d). Fluorescent is one of the most fascinating features of BPGQDs under UV light BPGQDs emitted bright green fluorescent. Additionally, the BPGQDs only have 2–5 layers of graphene in their structure, as shown by the height in the AFM picture, which ranges from 0.8 to 3.4 nm with an average of 2.2 nm Fig. 1(e) [42,43]. The as-prepared BPGQDs EDS spectra in Fig. S3 reveals that the C, O, B, and P concentrations are 22.78, 57.41,4.76 and 15 at%, respectively. This confirms the successful synthesis of co-doped BPGQDs. Functional group and chemical bonding of synthesized BPGQD was checked using FTIR spectroscopy.

In FTIR spectra we found more oxygen containing groups that proves the hydrophilic nature of BPGQDs a wide peak at 3470 cm−1 were owing to O–H stretching vibrations [44]. Peak around 1670 cm−1 and 2136 cm−1 were owing to Carbonyl group and C = C stretching vibrations respectively [45]. The peaks at 1416 cm−1 were due to B-O or C–C stretching vibrations [46] and peak at 1026 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1 were due to P-O and C-O stretching vibrations as shown in Fig. 1(f). The Quantum yield of synthesized BPGQDs were 27.9 % respective to reference Quinine Sulphate as shown in Fig.S4.

X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to further establish the elemental compositions and contents of the BPGQDs. The BPGQDs XPS survey spectra reveal that they have four peaks due to oxygen, carbon, boron and phosphorus at 533, 285,192 and 135 eV shown in Fig. 2 (a). The four peaks centred at 189.9,191.6, 192.3 and 194 eV in B1s high resolution spectrum were due to the B-C-O, B-C, B-O and BC2O respectively Fig. 2(b) [47]. From high resolution spectra of C1s we found 3 peaks at 284.6, 285.2 and 287.8 eV due to C-B, O-C-O and C-P bond respectively shown in Fig. 2(c). High scan spectra of O1s showed 3 three peaks at 531.8 eV and 533.5 eV and 533.1 eV were attribute to O-C-O, P-O and C = O respectively shown in Fig. 2(d) [48]. The high-resolution spectrum of phosphorus shown two peaks at 134.2 and 135.4 eV that attribute to the C-O-P and C-PO3 Fig. 2(e). These XPS result proves the successful synthesis of BPGQDs [49].

Variation in pH of the solution effects the intensity and it play great role in sensing because in basic condition NEP oxidized to its quinone form. For this, we prepared various pH buffer solution from 2 to 13 and then, recorded the fluorescence spectra at various pH value shown in Fig.3 (a). We observed the maximum PL intensity at pH value 7–8. The BPGQDs fluorescent intensity in buffer solution of pH 8.0 is shown to be relatively high [50]. The emission spectra of BPGQDs were observed by varying various excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm shown in Fig.3(b) and we found with increase in excitation wavelength from 300 to 340 nm emission intensity increase after that rise in excitation value the peak intensity decrease hence the maximum excitation wavelength for BPGQDs were 340 nm [51]. The fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs was significantly reduced as the incubation period increased from 0 to 5 min after the addition of NEP and continued to change slowly after 5 min, as shown in Fig.S5. Therefore, we decided to identify NEP after 5 min. The synthesized BPGQDs are highly stable compared to other quantum dots. As shown in Fig.S6,S7 fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs remain same after continuous irradiation of UV light (150 W xenon lamp) in spectrofluorometer and around 92 % fluorescence still remained. The synthesized materials are highly stable at room temperature till 6 months.

After the optimization of assay, sensing study was performed using the newly developed fluorescence-based sensitivity detection method for NEP. When the NEP concentration was gradually increased, the fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs linearly decreased Fig.3(c). The stern-Volmer constant (KSV), calculated using the relation is connected and quantified with the fluorescence quenching sensitivity, based on the fluorescence quenching with the addition of various amounts of NEP.
I 0
/ I = 1 +
Q
K
sv
Where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of BPGQDs at 420 nm without and with the addition of NEP. The calculated stern–volmer value is 15.5 * 106M−1. The Limit of detection, Relative standard deviation and limit of quantification are 16.2 nM, 0.72 and 49 nM respectively in wide linear range of concentration of NEP from 0 to 75 nM.

In the follow-up tests, several investigations are conducted to learn more about the fluorescence quenching mechanism of BPGQDs toward the NEP. It is well known that under neutral and alkaline circumstances, catecholamine can quickly oxidize to its quinone counterpart [52,53]. In this study, NEP were dissolved in alkaline circumstances, which helped to oxidize the catechol group in NEP. The solution color changes to brown proves the oxidation of NEP. On addition of NEP to the synthesized BPGQDs the fluorescent intensity decrease continuously and quenching occur. These impacts on fluorescence quenching may result from the transfer of electron from BPGQDs to quinones and may be due to interactions without transfer of electron [54]. Under the ideal experimental conditions, the BPGQDs fluorescent probe response to NEP has been studied. The fluorescent intensity at 420 nm continuously decreased with addition of different concentrations of NEP Fig. 3(c). As seen in Fig.3(d), a strong linear correlation among F0/F and NEP concentration in linear range of 0–75 nM with R2 value 0.9894 and the limit of detection was 16.2 nM. Additionally, this fabricated nanosensor for detection of NEP are highly selective, low limit of detection and sensitive than already reported methods shown in Table S2. As demonstrated, the suggested probe has a low detection limit for NEP and large linear range of conc. Fig. 1(d) shows the spectral overlay between absorption band of NEP and excitation spectrum of BPGQDs that give us an idea of FRET, PET and Inner filter effect (IFE) [55–59]. With the support of time correlated single photon count we can measure average lifetime of BPGQDs and founds no changes in average lifetime of BPGQDs in presence and absence of NEP, indicating that the quenched emission of BPGQDs with NEP is not consistent with the FRET and PET principle. Hence the FRET and PET mechanism is ruled out here. As shown in Fig. 2(f) we observed no change in lifetime of BPGQDs with the addition of NEP, initially the lifetime of BPGQDs was 4.102 ns after addition of NEP lifetime remains same, hence the sensing mechanism was IFE [60,61]. On the surface of BPGQDs more oxygen containing group present that interact non-covalently with functional group of NEP (C = O, O–H and NH2) through stacking, electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interaction. Fig.3(e) shows the CIE Plot of BPGQDs in presence of NEP and give us an idea of change in color with addition of NEP to the BPGQDs. DFT study also favours our experimental mechanism and proves that no electron transfers possible between BPGQDs to NEP due to the large energy band gap between HOMO, LUMO of NEP and QDs and the sensing mechanism was IFE. DFT also explain that here quenching of intensity is possible rather than enhancement on the basis of HOMO, LUMO energy gap after interactions between BPGQDs and NEP.

Our experimental outcomes give an idea of no transfer of electron from fluorophore to quencher species and the mechanism of sensing is based on inner filter effects, electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bong and pi-pi stacking was present between NEP and BPGQDs. The experimental results also supported with the help of DFT studies by calculating HOMO-LUMO energies of BPGQDs and norepinephrine in Gaussian16 by using basics set 631G(d,p). HOMO-LUMO energy of BPGQDs are −12.77 eV and – 12.36 eV and for NEP are – 13.09 eV, and – 10.54 eV respectively. BPGQDs energy band gap is less than that of NEP as is obvious from HOMO-LUMO energy gaps that is 0.41 eV and 2.55 eV respectively leading to no electron transfer [62–65] from prior to latter shown in Fig. 4 and that’s also match with our results.

In order to study the formation of stable complex between BPGQDs and NEP. We calculated the optimized geometries and HOMO-LUMO energies of BPGQDs and complex BPGQDs + NEP using Gaussian16 shown in Fig.S8-S13. HOMO, LUMO energy gap of optimized BPGQDs and BPGQDs-NEP complex are 0.41eV and 0.19 eV respectively. Hence the result shows that the energy gap reduced after the complex formation that make the system more stable [66]. From literature we studied that if HOMO, LUMO energy gap of complex reduced than initial species than the possibility of fluorescence quenching and if HOMO, LUMO energy band gap increase than enhancement in fluorescence intensity observed [67]. Hence in our results energy gap reduced so, quenching occurred that also favour the fluorescence spectra. It is observed that more stable complexes are formed when BPGQDs interact with NEP as the overall energy of BPGQDs + NEP decreased as compared to free BPGQDs shown in table S1.

A number of species were chosen and the same detection method was applied to each of them in order to assess the suggested mechanism selectivity. It was assessed how well NEP could be detected selectively in comparison to other species including SER, DA.HCL, tryptophan, glycine, alanine, NaOH, urea, NaCl, ethylenediamine, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+ etc. The levels of all these species introduced were more than the levels of NEP added to the BPGQDs solution. No discernible alterations were seen for any other species, as illustrated in Fig. 5 . It confirms that the newly created fluorescence sensing mechanism is very selective for NEP Fig. 5(a). To test its intervention selectivity of BPGQDs the structural analogues of the detecting molecules were looked into under ideal circumstances. The fluorescence responses of BPGQDs were assessed in the presence of 30 nM NEP as well as 5-times greater concentrations of SER, DA.HCL, tryptophan, lysine, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+,glycine, alanine, NaOH, urea, NaCl, ethylenediamine etc. In comparison to other structural analogues with relative bigger quantities, the sensor showed a considerable selective fluorescence quenching for NEP. The presence of these structural counterparts had very negligible effects on the emission intensity of BPGQDs. So, the developed sensor is highly selectivity when exposed to NEP Fig. 5(b).

Portables and smartphone-based imaging recognition with ease of use are more accurate than naked eye recognition [68]. Solution of BPGQDs based nano sensors was carried out using RGB color detector software. When the photo was taken with an i-phone camera, the android app RGB (red, green, blue space) software analysed it simultaneously and displayed the outcomes on the mobile phone. Photo clicked with the camera as near as probable (6 cm distance) to the sample vessel without the use of flash. The gradual addition of NEP to BPGQD showed a color variation from green to blue under UV light shown in inset of Fig. 6 (b). RGB profiling was then used to measure color levels by camera, G values decreased further, R and B values increased with addition of NEP concentrations in the range of 0–70 µM, where the correlation coefficient was 0.9976. To establish a linear relationship of (R + G + B)/3 and NEP conc. we use NEP concentration as the ordinate and (R + G + B)/3 as abscissa. Further confirm the accuracy of the proposed smartphone detection method [69–71]. We adopted the average value of red, green, and blue to reduce the errors and improve the precision of the results shown in Fig. 6. Thus, NEP can be monitored quickly and easily with a smartphone equipped with a BPGQDs nanosensor. All the aforementioned results indicated that the developed smartphone recognition technique could sensitively detect NEP.

Using BPGQDs as a fluorescence probe, NEP concentrations in samples of human urine were determined in order to validate the precision and usability of the fluorescence approach. By mixing in various known concentrations of NEP. The spiked samples were made using urine samples by adding various known dosages of NEP. Table 1 shows that the typical assay recoveries of spiked NEP range from 99 % to 103 %, with an RSD of between 0 and 2 %. The outcomes showed that the technique may be used to find NEP in samples of human serum.

In conclusion, fabricated luminious graphene quantum dots co-doped with boron and phosphorus are highly sensitive and selective for the sensing of norepinephrine. NEP injection sequentially quenched the fluorescent emission of BPGQDs effectively. The fluorescence detection of NEP had a linear range from 0 to 75 nM and a LOD of 16.2 nM. The decrease in fluorescence of the fluorophore has been attributed to IFE mechanism between BPGQDs and NEP. TCSPC analysis shows no change in the lifetime of BPGQDs with or without the addition of NEP. To understand how NEP and BPGQDs interact and for exact quenching mechanism we used DFT. Our experimental results lead to the conclusion that there is no transfer of electron from fluorophore to quenchor species and the mechanism is IFE, which is also favoured by the calculation of LUMO and HOMO band gap in DFT using Gaussian16. A RGB color detector android smartphone based software was also used to efficiently detect NEP using BPGQDs nanosensor. The synthesized nanomaterials also detect NEP in the real samples. This method is inexpensive, simple and delivers decent results, so it have many advantages.

Jyoti Duhan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Himanshu Kumar: Writing – review & editing. Sangeeta Obrai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optlastec.2024.110665.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
